commands.review_summary:
The branch reclassifies Cloudflare-like 403 HTML provider responses as `upstream_html` and changes Codex ChatGPT model/WHAM usage requests to send browser-like User-Agent and browser request headers with focused tests.

PR surface: Source +33, Tests +11. Total +44 across 7 files.

Reproducibility: no. for the full live issue: I did not establish a live Codex OAuth model-call reproduction in this review. The classifier boundary is source-reproducible because current main maps detected status-403 HTML to `auth_html`, and the linked issue supplies the observed Cloudflare challenge text.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] Human handling remains because the contributor must supply live provider-path proof and maintainers must choose header mitigation versus classifier-only scope.

target.title: fix: browser-like Codex headers + Cloudflare challenge classification (#94432)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

OpenAI/Codex OAuth users can log in successfully but model calls fail with a misleading "Authentication failed" error. The real cause: `chatgpt.com/backend-api` returns an HTML Cloudflare browser-challenge page (HTTP 403) instead of an API response. The `openclaw/(os release; arch)` User-Agent and missing browser-standard headers trigger Cloudflare bot detection.

Issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/94432

## Why This Change Was Made

Three coordinated changes across all `chatgpt.com/backend-api` code paths:

1. **Cloudflare challenge classification.** The `classifyProviderRuntimeFailureKind` function treated ALL 403 HTML responses as `auth_html`, producing misleading "re-authenticate" prompts. Cloudflare browser-challenge pages are CDN/gateway blocks, not auth failures — they now return `upstream_html` with the accurate "CDN or gateway blocked" message. The detection pattern aligns with the shared Cloudflare detector in `src/shared/assistant-error-format.ts` (`STANDALONE_HTML_ERROR_HINT_RE`) to cover `cdn-cgi/challenge-platform`, `challenge-error-text`, `cloudflare`, `captcha`, `attention required` and more.

2. **Browser-like HTTP headers for Codex model calls.** Chrome-like User-Agent + standard browser headers (`Accept`, `Accept-Language`) replace the `openclaw/...` fingerprint on Codex model calls. The `buildBaseCodexHeaders` builder applies these to SSE, WebSocket, and regular HTTP paths, with `Accept` properly overridden by `buildSSEHeaders`/`buildWebSocketHeaders`.

3. **Browser-like headers for WHAM usage probes.** The Codex usage fetch and WHAM cooldown probe both call `chatgpt.com/backend-api/wham/usage`. The User-Agent override is applied **after** `resolveProviderRequestHeaders()` to bypass the OpenAI attribution policy (`provider-attribution.ts:528`) which injects `openclaw/...` UA during resolution.

## User Impact

- Codex OAuth users see accurate error messages (CDN/gateway block) instead of misleading auth-failure prompts
- Browser-like headers may reduce or eliminate Cloudflare blocks entirely
- WHAM quota probes share the same header contract as model calls — no inconsistent fingerprinting
- Generic 403 HTML still classified as `auth_html` — no regression for non-Cloudflare providers

## Evidence

**137 tests, 0 failures across 4 test suites:**

```
provider-error-patterns.test.ts:  40 passed (classification + edge cases)
openai-chatgpt-responses.test.ts: 14 passed (header builder contract)
provider-usage.fetch.codex.test.ts: 9 passed (post-attribution UA verified)
usage.test.ts:                     74 passed (WHAM probe header contract)
```

**Classification coverage:**
- Cloudflare challenge HTML (`cdn-cgi/challenge-platform`, `Enable JavaScript and cookies to continue`) + 403 → `upstream_html`
- Generic 403 HTML (plain "Forbidden") + 403 → `auth_html` (no regression)
- 8 Cloudflare markers aligned with shared detector

**Header contract verification (mock-level):**
- `fetchCodexUsage` → Mozilla UA present on outbound request
- `probeWhamForCooldown` → Mozilla UA present on outbound request
- `buildBaseCodexHeaders` → Mozilla UA + Accept + Accept-Language on all transport paths

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Codex OAuth calls to chatgpt.com/backend-api blocked by Cloudflare browser-detection; errors misleadingly blamed authentication.

**Real setup tested**: Linux x86_64 (kernel 4.19.112), Node.js v24.13.1, openclaw main @ 41691a82d5.

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run   src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers/provider-error-patterns.test.ts   src/llm/providers/openai-chatgpt-responses.test.ts   src/infra/provider-usage.fetch.codex.test.ts   src/agents/auth-profiles/usage.test.ts
```

**After-fix evidence**: 137 tests pass. Classification correctly distinguishes Cloudflare challenge pages from auth failures. Browser-like headers are set on all three `chatgpt.com/backend-api` paths post-attribution-policy.

**What was not tested**: Live Codex OAuth session — this environment cannot reach chatgpt.com (corporate network restriction). The header change follows the same pattern used by `web_fetch.ts` (Chrome-like User-Agent for Cloudflare-protected endpoints). Codex upstream source (`codex-rs/login/src/auth/default_client.rs`) uses Codex default headers + a Cloudflare cookie store, so browser-like headers are a plausible transport-level workaround. If Cloudflare blocks persist, a follow-up could explore TLS fingerprint impersonation via undici dispatcher options.

## Risk checklist

- [x] Backward-compatible: generic 403 HTML still `auth_html`
- [x] `Accept` overridden by SSE/WebSocket builders — no conflict
- [x] `Accept-Language` additive only — no conflict
- [x] Cloudflare markers aligned with shared detector (`STANDALONE_HTML_ERROR_HINT_RE`)
- [x] WHAM probe + usage fetch share same browser-like header contract
- [x] No new dependencies
target.author: sunlit-deng
